Exploratory Data Analysis: Factors Influencing IMDb Movie Quality

Author

Group 10

Published

March 19, 2026

1 Introduction

The following analysis explores the internal characteristics of the movie dataset to identify potential predictors for our Generalized Linear Model (GLM). We specifically focus on whether a movie achieves an IMDb rating greater than 7.

2 Exploratory Analysis

2.1 Data Summary & Descriptive Statistics

To understand the central tendency and dispersion of our movie features, we generated a summary table for numerical variables. As presented in Table 1, the dataset covers a wide range of years and budgets.

The diversity of movie genres is another key aspect of our dataset. Table 2 shows the count and percentage of movies within each genre category after cleaning the missing values.

By reviewing Table 1, the variance of votes is exceptionally high compared to other predictors. This extreme dispersion indicates a “long-tail” distribution where a few extremely popular films receive a disproportionate amount of audience engagement, illustrating the need for a further transformation to stabilize the variable for the GLM. Furthermore, Table 2 highlights that Drama and Action are the most two prevalent categories in our sample. In contrast, Romance represents the smallest segment of the sample. This distribution reflects the producer’s focus on mainstream genres but suggests that the model’s predictive power for specific genres like Romance may be more limited.

2.2 Class Distribution

We examine the balance of our target variable (movie quality). As shown in Figure 1, we can observe the frequency of movies categorized as “Good” versus “Poor”.

Based on Figure 1, a significant class imbalance is observed in the target variable, with ‘Poor’ films (\(Rating < 7\)) outnumbering ‘Good’ films (\(Rating > 7\)) by nearly a factor of two. Because the model sees more examples of ‘Poor’ films, it might become better at identifying failures than predicting successes.

2.3 Numerical Variables vs Target

Analysis of the numerical predictors in Figure 2 reveals that while Year and Budget show nearly identical distributions across quality classes, Length and Votes offer more significant insights. Notably, “Poor” movies exhibit a “Mediocrity Trap,” with their durations tightly clustered around a standardized 100-minute mark, yet plagued by numerous extreme outliers (represented by the red markers in Figure 2). In contrast, “Good” movies—while also averaging near 100 minutes—display a much more flexible and varied distribution. Furthermore, because both the “Good” and “Poor” categories exhibit extreme right-skewness in their raw audience engagement metrics, we utilized a logarithmic transformation for the Votes variable. As shown in the red-highlighted outliers, this step was essential to stabilize the variance.

To address the extreme right-skewness inherent in audience engagement metrics, we compared the raw distribution of votes against a log-transformed version. As shown in Figure 3, the original votes variable is heavily compressed near zero with an expansive long tail, which would disproportionately bias our GLM. By applying a logarithmic transformation, we successfully achieved a more bell-shaped, stable distribution (Right), ensuring that the variance is consistent across both quality categories and making the data suitable for parametric modeling.

2.4 Categorical Analysis of Genre

As illustrated in Figure 4, genres such as Documentary and Short appear to have a significantly higher probability of being rated above 7.0 compared to Action or Drama films.

2.5 Multi-collinearity Check & Correlation

As shown in Figure 5, no predictors exhibit a correlation higher than 0.7, confirming that we can include all variables in the GLM.

3 Summary of Analysis

Our exploratory analysis shows several patterns in the data. First, there is a clear class imbalance: “Poor” films appear about twice as often as “Good” films, which creates a strong baseline for prediction. We also find that budget and production year have little relationship with film ratings. However, film length shows an interesting pattern. Lower-rated films tend to cluster around 100 minutes, while higher-rated films have a wider range of lengths.

In addition, the number of audience votes is highly right-skewed, so we applied a log transformation to stabilize the variance. After this transformation, higher-rated films still show consistently higher audience engagement. By combining these findings with differences across genres, we gain a clearer understanding of the factors related to movie quality and build a stronger foundation for our GLM model.

4 Formal Data Analysis

Based on the exploratory analysis, we now fit a formal Generalised Linear Model (GLM) to investigate which movie characteristics are associated with a higher probability of receiving an IMDb rating above 7.

4.1 Model Setup

The response variable for the formal analysis is is_high_rating, which equals 1 if a movie has a rating above 7 and 0 otherwise. Since the response is binary, a logistic regression model is appropriate.

Based on the exploratory analysis, the predictors included in the model are year, length, budget, log_votes, and genre. The reference category for genre is set to Comedy, so that the coefficients of other genres can be interpreted relative to this baseline.

To improve model stability, rare genre categories are grouped into an “Other” category, and vote counts are log-transformed to reduce skewness. The final dataset contains 1436 observations after preprocessing.

4.2 Logistic Regression Model

To investigate the determinants of a movie’s likelihood of receiving a high rating, we employed a Logistic Regression model. Given the potential for quasi-complete separation in categorical data, we utilized the Firth’s penalized maximum likelihood estimation (via the brglmFit method) to ensure parameter stability and reduce bias. \[ \ln\left( \frac{P}{1 - P} \right) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \text{year} + \beta_2 \text{length} + \beta_3 \text{budget} + \beta_4 \text{log\_votes} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \gamma_i \text{genre}_i \]

Where:

  • \(P\): The probability that the outcome variable is_high_rating equals 1 (representing a high-rated movie).
  • \(\text{log\_votes}\): The natural logarithm of the vote count, used to normalize the distribution and reduce the impact of extreme outliers.
  • \(\text{genre}\): A categorical predictor where rare genres have been grouped into an “Other” category to improve model stability.
  • \(\beta_i\): The estimated coefficients for the continuous and discrete numerical predictors.
  • \(\gamma_i\): The estimated coefficients for the specific movie genre categories.
  • \(\ln\left( \frac{P}{1 - P} \right)\): The log-odds (logit), which represents the link between the linear predictors and the probability of the outcome.

Call:
glm(formula = is_high_rating ~ year + length + budget + log_votes + 
    genre, family = binomial(link = "logit"), data = model_data, 
    method = "brglmFit")

Deviance Residuals: 
    Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max  
-3.1637  -0.3487  -0.0878   0.2396   3.1268  

Coefficients:
                  Estimate Std. Error z value             Pr(>|z|)    
(Intercept)      -9.867378   8.262558  -1.194               0.2324    
year              0.004981   0.004215   1.182               0.2373    
length           -0.073605   0.005923 -12.428 < 0.0000000000000002 ***
budget            0.553001   0.042969  12.870 < 0.0000000000000002 ***
log_votes         0.124864   0.057631   2.167               0.0303 *  
genreAction      -3.087399   0.254398 -12.136 < 0.0000000000000002 ***
genreAnimation   -4.423899   0.580786  -7.617   0.0000000000000259 ***
genreDocumentary  2.326001   0.501552   4.638   0.0000035246096084 ***
genreDrama       -5.268111   0.399279 -13.194 < 0.0000000000000002 ***
genreOther       -2.233248   0.530956  -4.206   0.0000259829782631 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 1854.97  on 1435  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance:  737.47  on 1426  degrees of freedom
AIC:  757.47

Type of estimator: AS_mixed (mixed bias-reducing adjusted score equations)
Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 4

An initial model including year was fitted. However, as year was not statistically significant (p > 0.05), it was removed to improve model parsimony.

So our final model would be: \[ \ln\left( \frac{P}{1 - P} \right) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \text{length} + \beta_2 \text{budget} + \beta_3 \text{log\_votes} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \gamma_i \text{genre}_i \]

Table 3 indicates that several predictors are significantly associated with the probability of receiving a rating above 7.

Higher budgets and greater audience engagement (log_votes) increase the likelihood of a high rating, while longer movie duration reduces it.

Genre also plays a substantial role: compared with Comedy, Documentary films have markedly higher odds of receiving high ratings, whereas Animation and Drama are associated with lower odds.

4.3 Model Coefficients Table

Table 4 reports the estimated odds ratios, standard errors and confidence intervals for all predictors in the final logistic regression model.

4.4 Predicted Probability Plot

To visualise the fitted model, Figure 6 shows the predicted probability of a movie receiving a rating above 7 across different levels of audience engagement (log_votes), while holding other variables fixed at their median values.

Besides, we can also noticed that the probability of a high rating increases with audience engagement. The approximately parallel curves indicate that the effect of log_votes is consistent across genres, with differences primarily reflected in baseline probabilities.

Figure 7 presents the estimated odds ratios and confidence intervals for each predictor.

The plot highlights the magnitude of effects on the odds scale. Documentary films have odds of high ratings more than 10 times that of Comedy, whereas Animation and Drama have substantially lower odds. Continuous predictors such as budget and log_votes increase the odds of high ratings, while movie length reduces them.

4.5 Conclusion

This study identifies audience engagement, budget, and genre as key determinants of high movie ratings. Movies with higher vote counts and larger budgets are significantly more likely to receive ratings above 7, whereas longer duration is associated with reduced likelihood.

Substantial differences across genres persist after controlling for other variables, highlighting the independent role of genre in shaping perceived movie quality.

Overall, the fitted logistic regression model offers a stable and interpretable framework for explaining variation in IMDb ratings.
